Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## QUASI-NEWTON POLICY GRADIENT ALGORITHMS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Policy gradient algorithms have been widely applied to reinforcement learning (RL) problems in recent years. Regularization with various entropy functions is often used to encourage exploration and improve stability. In this paper, we propose a quasi-Newton method for the policy gradient algorithm with entropy regularization. In the case of Shannon entropy, the resulting algorithm reproduces the natural policy gradient (NPG) algorithm. For other entropy functions, this method results in brand new policy gradient algorithms. We provide a simple proof that all these algorithms enjoy the Newton-type quadratic convergence near the optimal policy. Using synthetic and industrial-scale examples, we demonstrate that the proposed quasi-Newton method typically converges in single-digit iterations, often orders of magnitude faster than other state-of-the-art algorithms.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Consider an infinite-horizon MDP (Bellman, 1957; Sutton & Barto, 2018) _M_ = ( _S, A, P, r, γ_ ), where _S_ is a set of states of the system studied, _A_ is a set of actions made by the agent, _P_ is a transition probability tensor with _Pst[a]_[being][the][probability][of][transitioning][from][state] _[s]_[to][state] _[t]_ when taking action _a_ , _r_ is a reward tensor with _rs[a]_[being][the][reward][obtained][when][taking][action] _a_ at state _s_ , and 0 _< γ <_ 1 is a discount factor. Throughout the paper, the state space _S_ and the action space _A_ are assumed to be finite. A policy _π_ is a randomized rule of action-selection where _πs[a]_[denotes the probability of choosing action] _[ a]_[ at state] _[ s]_[.][For a given policy] _[ π]_[, the value function] _vπ_ defined as

satisfies the Bellman equation:

where ( _Pπ_ ) _st_ =[�] _a[π] s[a][P][ a] st_[,][ (] _[r][π]_[)] _[s]_[=][ �] _a[π] s[a][r] s[a]_[, and] _[ I]_[is the identity operator.] In order to promote exploration and enhance stability, one often regularizes the problem with a function _hπ_ such as the negative Shannon entropy (˜ _hπ_ ) _s_ =[�] _a[π] s[a]_[log] _[ π] s[a]_[.][With] _[h][π]_[,][the][original] reward _rπ_ is replaced with the regularized reward _rπ_ = _rπ − τhπ_ where _τ_ is the regularization coefficient and (2) becomes

where we overload the notation _v_ for the regularized value function. Other continuously differentiable entropy functions can be used as well, as we will show later. Since _γ <_ 1 and _Pπ_ is a transition probability matrix, ( _I − γPπ_ ) is invertible, and
In a policy optimization problem, we seek a policy _π_ that maximizes _e[⊤] vπ_ for some (in fact arbitrary) positive weight vector _e ∈_ R _[|][S][|]_ . Using (4), the problem can be stated as

This problem can be solved by, for example, the policy gradient (PG) method. However, the vanilla PG method converges quite slowly. In Agarwal et al. (2020), for example, the vanilla PG method is shown to have a _O_ ( _T[−]_[1] ) convergence rate, where _T_ denotes the number of iterations. For the

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

PG method with entropy regularization and some of its variants, the convergence rate can be improved to _O_ ( _e[−][cT]_ ), i.e., linear convergence (Mei et al., 2020), which can still be slow since _c_ is in general close to 0. It is also demonstrated in numerical examples that these algorithms with linear convergence rate can suffer from slow convergence. For example, in the example in Zhan et al. (2021), thousands of iterations are needed for the algorithm to converge, even though the model is relatively small and sparse. Therefore, there is a clear need for designing new methods with faster convergence and one idea is to take the geometry of the problem into consideration. The Newton method, for example, preconditions the gradient with the Hessian matrix and obtains second-order local convergence. Since the exact Hessian matrix is usually too computationally expensive to obtain, the quasi-Newton type methods, which use structurally simpler approximations of the Hessian instead, are more widely used in generic optimization problems, and are known to enjoy superlinear convergence (Rodomanov & Nesterov, 2021a;b).

## 1.1 CONTRIBUTIONS

In this paper, we investigate the quasi-Newton approach for solving (5). The main contributions of this paper are the following.

- First, we present a unified quasi-Newton type method for the policy optimization problem. The main observation is to decompose the Hessian as a sum of a diagonal part and a remainder, where at the optimal solution the remainder part vanishes so that the Hessian becomes diagonal. This inspires us to use only the diagonal part in the quasi-Newton method. As a result, the proposed method not only leverage the second order information but also enjoys low computational cost due to the diagonal structure of the preconditioner used. When the negative Shannon entropy is used, this method reproduces the natural policy gradient (NPG) algorithm. For other entropic regularizations, this method results in brand new policy gradient algorithms.

- Second, we analyze the convergence property of the proposed quasi-Newton algorithms and demonstrate local quadratic convergence both theoretically and numerically. By leveraging the quasi-Newton framework (Dennis & Mor´e, 1974), we provide a simple and straightforward proof for quadratic convergence near the optimal policy. In the numerical tests, we verify that the proposed method leads to fast quadratic convergence even under small regularization and large discount rate (close to 1). Even for industrial-size problems with hundreds of thousands states, the quasi-Newton method converges in single-digit iterations and within a few minutes on a regular laptop.

## 1.2 BACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK

A major workhorse behind the recent success in reinforcement learning (RL) is the large family of policy gradient (PG) methods (Williams, 1992; Sutton et al., 1999), for example, the natural policy gradient (NPG) method (Kakade, 2001), the actor-critic method (Konda & Tsitsiklis, 2000), the asynchronous advantage actor-critic (A3C) method (Mnih et al., 2016), the deterministic policy gradient (DPG) method (Silver et al., 2014), the trust region policy optimization (TRPO) (Schulman et al., 2015a), the generalized advantage estimation (GAE) (Schulman et al., 2015b), and proximal policy optimization (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017), to mention but a few. The NPG method is known to be drastically faster than the original PG method, intuitively because in the NPG method the policy gradient is preconditioned by the Fisher information (an approximation of the Hessian of the KL-divergence) matrix and fits the problem geometry better. This idea is extended in TRPO and PPO where the problem geometry is taken into consideration via trust region constraints (in terms of KL-divergence) and a clipping function of the relative ratio of policies in the objective function, respectively. These implicit ways (in the sense that they do not adjust the gradient by an explicit preconditioner) of adjusting the policy gradient is in essence similar to the mirror descent (MD) method (Nemirovskij & Yudin, 1983) in generic optimization problems.

This similarity in addressing the inherent geometry of the problem is noticed by a line of recent work including Neu et al. (2017); Geist et al. (2019); Shani et al. (2020); Tomar et al. (2020); Lan (2021), and the analysis techniques in MD methods have been adapted to the PG setting. The connection was first built explicitly in Neu et al. (2017). The authors consider a linear program formulation where the objective function is the average reward and the domain is the set of stationary state-action

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

distributions, in which case the TRPO method can be viewed as an approximate mirror descent method and the A3C method as an MD method for the dual-averaging (Nesterov, 2009) objective. As a complement, Geist et al. (2019) considers an actor-critic type method where the policy is updated via either a regularized greedy step or an MD step and the value function is updated by a regularized Bellman operator, which also includes TRPO as a special case, and error propagation analysis is provided. In Shani et al. (2020), an adaptive scaling that naturally arises in the policy gradient is applied to the proximity term of the MD formulation, and sublinear convergence result is proved with a properly decreasing learning rate. In Tomar et al. (2020), the application to the nontabular setting is enabled by parameterizing the policy and applying MD to the policy parameters, and the corresponding sublinear convergence result is presented.

Regularization, a strategy that considers the modified objective function with an additional penalty term on the policy, is another crucial component in the development of PG type methods. Intuitively, regularization is able to encourage exploration in the policy iteration process and thus avoid local minima. It is also suggested (Ahmed et al., 2019) that regularization makes the optimization landscape smoother and thus enables possibly faster convergence. Linear convergence result is then established for regularized PG and NPG methods (Agarwal et al., 2020; Mei et al., 2020; Cen et al., 2020). In these relatively earlier works (Agarwal et al., 2020; Mei et al., 2020; Cen et al., 2020), the regularization usually takes the form of (negative) entropy or relative entropy. In the more recent work Lan (2021) and Zhan et al. (2021) that follow the MD type methods, the regularization is extended to general convex functions with the resulting Bregman divergences different from the KL-divergence and linear convergence is guaranteed as well.

However, most of these algorithms are of either sublinear or linear convergence except the entropy regularized NPG with full step length (which is a special case of the quasi-Newton method we propose), and even the linear convergence rate _O_ ( _e[−][cT]_ ) can be slow since _c_ can be close to zero. This motivates us to invent the quasi-Newton policy gradient method to be introduced in Section 2.

## 2 QUASI-NEWTON METHOD

This section derives the quasi-Newton method for the entropic-regularized policy optimization problems. In this paper, we use a more general definition of the term “quasi-Newton method”. Unlike classical quasi-Newton methods where the approximate Hessian matrix is constructed using firstorder information, the proposed quasi-Newton method uses second-order information, but the approximate Hessian matrix remains simple and its inverse is easy to obtain. We start with the negative Shannon entropy ( _hπ_ ) _s_ =[�] _a[π] s[a]_[log] _[ π] s[a]_[.]

## 3 QUADRATIC CONVERGENCE

In this section, we study the quadratic convergence of the quasi-Newton method at learning rate _η_ = 1. Our analysis is inspired by the results in Dennis & Mor´e (1974); Wang & Yan (2021). The following theorem states the second-order convergence when _η_ = 1, with the proof given in Appendix E.

**Connection with mirror descent** The quasi-Newton algorithm (17) for _η_ = 1 has a deep connection with mirror descent. The vanilla mirror descent of _−E_ ( _π_ ) with a learning rate _β_ and the Bregman divergence associated with Φ is given by

In Zhan et al. (2021), a variant of mirror descent is proposed based on an implicit update scheme

In the next section, we will compare this variant with our quasi-Newton method (17) in several examples and show that the quasi-Newton method converges orders of magnitudes faster than the ones in Zhan et al. (2021).

## 4 NUMERICAL EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 EXPERIMENT I

We first test the quasi-Newton methods derived in Section 2 on the model in Zhan et al. (2021). For the sake of completeness, we include the model description here. The MDP considered has a state space _S_ of size 200 and an action space _A_ of size 50. For each state _t_ and action _a_ , a subset _St[a]_[of] _[ S]_ is uniformly randomly chosen such that _|St[a][|]_[ = 20][, and] _[ P][ a] tt[′]_[= 1] _[/]_[20][ for any] _[ t][′][∈][S] t[a]_[.][The reward is] given by _rs[a]_[=] _[ U] s[ a][U][s]_[, where] _[ U] s[ a]_[and] _[ U][s]_[are independently uniformly chosen on][ [0] _[,]_[ 1]][.][The discount] rate _γ_ is set as 0 _._ 99 and the regularization coefficient _τ_ = 0 _._ 001.

In the numerical experiment, we implement Algorithm 1 with the KL divergence, the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence with _α_ = _−_ 3. We set the initial policy as the uniform policy, the convergence threshold as _ϵ_ = 10 _[−]_[12] and the learning rate _η_ as 1. Figure 1(a) demonstrates that, for these four tests, the quasi-Newton algorithm converges in 7, 7, 7, and 6 iterations, respectively. In comparison, we apply the policy mirror descent (PMD) and the general policy mirror descent (GPMD) method in Zhan et al. (2021) to the same MDP with the same stopping criterion. As also shown in Figure 1(a), many more iterations are needed for GPMD and PMD to reach the same precision: GPMD converges in 14822 iterations, and PMD does not reach the desired precision after 3 _×_ 10[5] iterations.

In order to verify the quadratic convergence proved in Section 3, we draw the plots of log _|_ log _∥π − π[∗] ∥F |_ in Figures 1(b), 1(c), 1(d) and 1(e), where _π[∗]_ is the final policy and the norm used is the Frobenius norm. A green reference line with slope log 2 through the origin is plotted for comparison. If the error converges exactly at a quadratic rate, the plot of log _|_ log _∥π − π[∗] ∥|_ shall be parallel to the reference line. The convergence curves approach the reference lines in the end (and are even steeper than the reference lines in the begining), demonstrating clearly a quadratic convergence for all regularizations used here.

## 4.2 EXPERIMENT II

Next, we apply the quasi-Newton methods derived in Section 2 to an MDP model constructed from the search logs of an online shopping store, with two different ranking strategies. Each issued query is represented as a state in the MDP. In response to a query, the search can be done by choosing one of the two ranking strategies (actions) to return a ranked list of products shown to the customer. Based on the shown products, the customer can refine or update the query, thus entering a new state. The reward at each state-action pair is a weighted sum of the clicks and purchases resulting from the action. Based on the data collected from two separate 5-week periods for both ranking strategies, we construct an MDP with 135k states, and a very sparse transition tensor _P_ with only 0 _._ 01% nonzero entries. The discount rate _γ_ is set as 0 _._ 99 and the regularization coefficient is _τ_ = 0 _._ 001.

When calculating _vπ_ by _vπ_ = ( _I − γPπ_ ) _[−]_[1] ( _rπ − τhπ_ ), we apply the iterative solver Bi-CGSTAB (Van der Vorst, 1992), a widely used numerical method with high efficiency and robustness for

7

In the numerical experiment, we implement Algorithm 1 with the KL divergence, the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence with _α_ = _−_ 3. We set the initial policy as the uniform policy, the convergence threshold as _ϵ_ = 10 _[−]_[12] and the learning rate _η_ as 1. All the tests end up with fast convergence as shown in Figure 2(a), where logarithmic scale is used for the vertical axis. More specifically, the quasi-Newton algorithm using the KL divergence, the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence with _α_ = _−_ 3 converge in 6 _,_ 6 _,_ 6 _,_ 5 iterations, respectively. It is worth noticing that even though the size of the state space _S_ here is some magnitudes larger than the examples in Section 4.1, the number of quasi-Newton iterations used is about the same.

In Table 1, we report the number of BiCGSTAB steps used in the algorithm. In each quasi-Newton iteration, less than 20 BiCGSTAB steps are used in order to find _vπ_ . For all the 4 regularizers used here, altogether only about 100 BiCGSTAB steps are needed in the whole training process, thanks to the fast convergence of the quasi-Newton method.

As in the previous numerical example, in Figure 2(b), 2(c), 2(d) and 2(e) we verify the quadratic convergence by comparing the plot of log _|_ log _∥π − π[∗] ∥|_ with a green reference line through the origin with slope log 2. As the convergence curves are approximately parallel to the reference lines, this verifies that the proposed algorithm converges quadratically with all the regularizations in this example as well.

## 5 DISCUSSION

In this paper, we present a fast quasi-Newton method for the policy gradient algorithm that is capable of solving large MDP problems with large ( _≈_ 1) discount rate and small ( _≈_ 0) regularizations. The proposed method includes the well-known natural policy gradient algorithm as a special case, and naturally extends to other regularizers such as the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence.

Theoretically, we show local quadratic convergence of the proposed quasi-Newton method with a relatively simple proof. This quadratic convergence is confirmed numerically on both medium and large sparse models. In contrast with mirror descent type methods (e.g. Zhan et al. (2021)) that take up to tens of thousands iterations even with manually tuned learning rate, the proposed quasiNewton algorithms typically converge in less than 10 iterations, despite the large discount rate (close to 1) and small regularization coefficient ( _τ ≈_ 0).

For future work, we plan to adapt the technique used here to other gradient based algorithms for solving the MDP problems. Other types of _f_ -divergence can also be included. Another direction is to consider continuous MDP problems by leveraging function approximation, effective spatial discretization, or model reduction.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel quasi-Newton framework for regularized policy gradients that achieves quadratic convergence by approximating the Hessian with a diagonal matrix that becomes exact at the optimum. The methodology is technically sound, and the connection to Natural Policy Gradient and Accelerated Mirror Descent provides strong theoretical grounding. However, there are minor technical inconsistencies in the parameterization of the alpha-divergence and the derivation of the update rules (specifically the coefficients and signs in Theorem 2 and Remark 2) that could confuse a reader attempting to reproduce the results. The numerical results are impressive, demonstrating significant speedups over first-order methods.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Alpha-Divergence Update Formula
comments.explanation: In Theorem 2, the authors define the parameterization \theta = \phi'(|A|\pi). In the Shannon entropy case (Theorem 1), the derivative $\phi'(x) = \log(x) + 1$. However, the general update rule in equation (14) includes a trailing $-\tau$ inside the parentheses $(r_{s[a]} - \tau - ...)$. While this constant term can theoretically be absorbed into the Lagrange multiplier $c_s$ for the Shannon case, its explicit presence in the 'unified' formula (14) is confusing for general $\alpha$-divergences where the derivative of the regularizer doesn't necessarily produce a additive constant 1 in the same way. A careful reader might be unsure if the $-\tau$ is an artifact of the KL derivation or a necessary component of the general quasi-Newton step.

comments.title: Potential Sign/Coefficient Error in Hellinger Update
comments.explanation: In Remark 2, the expression for the Hellinger update ($\alpha=0$) contains $(\dots)^{-2}$. Given that $\phi(x) = (1-\sqrt{x})^2$ or similar for Hellinger, $\phi'(x)$ involves $1 - x^{-1/2}$. Inverting this to find the policy update usually results in a power of $-2$. However, the OCR/text in Remark 2: 'sqrt(1 - pi eta_{s[a]} - eta ...)' contains symbols like 'pi' and 'eta' inside the square root that appear to be typos or OCR errors for the previous policy iteration $\pi^{(k)}$ or specific rewards. More importantly, the use of $\pi$ inside the expression for updating $\pi$ suggests a specific recursive form, but the notation $\pi \eta_{s[a]}$ is undefined. Checking against equation (14), the update should solve $\phi'(\pi^{new}) = \text{RHS}$. For Hellinger, $\phi'(x) \propto 1 - x^{-1/2}$, so $x \propto (1 - \text{RHS})^{-2}$. The term inside the square root in the paper is likely a typo for $(\phi'(\pi^{(k)}))^{1/2}$ or similar.

comments.title: Subscript indices in Hessian definition
comments.explanation: In the definition of the diagonal Hessian $\tilde{H}$ just before equation (10), the expression ends with a subscript 's' outside the parentheses: $(w_\pi \pi_{s[a]})_s$. Since $w_\pi$ is already defined as a vector in $R^{|S|}$, and $\pi_{s[a]}$ is a scalar, the outer subscript 's' is redundant or mathematically ambiguous. If it denotes the $s$-th component of a vector, then $\pi_{s[a]}$ shouldn't have indices. It is clear that the authors intend $\tilde{H}$ to be $\text{diag}(w_s \cdot \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial \pi^2})$, but the notation here is slightly cluttered.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for Terminal State substitution
comments.quote: In that case we can remove $s_T$ from $S$ and replace $\gamma$ with 1 in the update schemes.
comments.explanation: In Remark 4, the authors suggest the algorithm works for $\gamma=1$ by simply removing the terminal state. While this is a common practice in episodic MDPs to convert them to transient MDPs, the quadratic convergence proof in Theorem 5 relies on the invertibility of $(I - \gamma P_\pi)$ and properties of the regularized value function. When $\gamma=1$, the spectral radius of $P_{\pi}$ is exactly 1. Although removing the terminal state makes the sub-matrix $\tilde{P}$ transient (spectral radius < 1), the transition from the discounted case to the undiscounted case in terms of the Hessian decomposition $\tilde{H} + H_r$ is non-trivial and technically requires verifying that $H_r$ still vanishes at the same rate. The prose glosses over whether the quadratic convergence constant $C$ is affected of the conditioning of $(I - \tilde{P})$.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling quasi-Newton approach to policy gradient methods with entropy regularization, unifying existing methods like natural policy gradients and extending them to general f-divergences. The theoretical justification for local quadratic convergence is elegant and well-structured, drawing on classical optimization theory. The numerical results on both synthetic and real-world-scale problems convincingly demonstrate exceptional speed, converging in single-digit iterations. While the core ideas are strong and the algorithmic framework is innovative, there are several technical inconsistencies—particularly in the definition of the α-divergence and the parameterization used in updates—that affect reproducibility and theoretical clarity. These issues are moderate in severity but could mislead a careful reader attempting to implement or extend the method.

comments.title: Incorrect α-divergence formula and sign inconsistency
comments.explanation: The expression for the α-divergence in (12) appears to contain multiple OCR or author errors. First, the factor '4' before the sum seems spurious; standard α-divergence definitions do not have this. Second, the function φ(x) = 1 − 4α²(1 − x^{(1+α)/2}) does not match standard forms. Typically, φ-divergence for α-divergence uses φ(x) = 4/(1−α²)(1 − x^{(1+α)/2}) for α ≠ ±1, which ensures proper normalization and recovers KL and reverse-KL in limits. The current expression fails dimensional consistency—φ(x) should be nonnegative and convex, but this form may not be. Furthermore, the sign in (12) suggests (hπ)_s = |A|(1−α²)∑(1 − (|A|π_s[a])^{(1+α)/2}), which is negative for π_s[a] < 1, contradicting the usual definition of entropy as nonpositive. This contradicts the claim that α=0 gives Hellinger divergence, which is positive. The issue is not resolved by context, as later algorithm steps depend on this definition. Even allowing OCR errors, the expression cannot be reconciled with standard forms without significant reinterpretation.

comments.title: Mismatch between φ′ in Theorem 2 and claimed α-divergence
comments.explanation: In Theorem 2, the update rule (14) uses θ_s[a] = φ′(|A|π_s[a]), with φ defined as φ(x) = 1 − 4α²(1 − x^{(1+α)/2}). However, the derivative φ′(x) = −4α² ⋅ (−(1+α)/2) x^{(α−1)/2} = 2α²(1+α)x^{(α−1)/2}, which does not match standard dual parameterizations for α-exponential families. For example, when α=0, this gives φ′(x) = 0, which is invalid. But α=0 should correspond to Hellinger, where θ_s[a] ∝ √π_s[a], implying φ′(x) ∝ 1/√x. The claimed update in Remark 2 for α=0 (π_new ∝ (√π + η(·))²) supports this expectation. Therefore, the stated φ(x) cannot yield the correct parameterization. The mismatch undermines the consistency of the general framework: either φ is wrong or the update is misderived. This is not an OCR issue, as the function is used consistently (but incorrectly) throughout.

comments.title: Incorrect exponent in Algorithm 1 for α-divergence update
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, line 12 sets σ = 2/(α−1), but then uses (c_s + x_a)^σ in line 15 to update π_new. For α-divergence, the dual variable is θ_s[a] = φ′(|A|π_s[a]) ∝ (|A|π_s[a])^{(α−1)/2}, so π_s[a] ∝ θ_s[a]^{2/(α−1)}. Thus, the exponent should be 2/(α−1), which matches σ. However, when α < 1 (as specified), α−1 < 0, so σ < 0, meaning π_s[a] = (c_s + x_a)^σ blows up as c_s + x_a → 0. But in Remark 2, for α=0, the update is π_s[a] ∝ (·)^{-2}, which would be unstable. More critically, for the reverse-KL case (α→−1), σ → 2/(−2) = −1, so π_s[a] ∝ (·)^{-1}, but Remark 2 says it should be (·)^{-1}, which matches. However, the standard reverse-KL update uses log, not inversion. The deeper issue is that the update π_new = (c_s + x_a)^σ does not preserve the normalization unless the bisection in line 14 accounts for this exponent correctly. The algorithm uses Lemma 3 with general σ < 0, but the convergence and feasibility depend critically on the exponent. The inconsistency between the expected behavior of α-divergence and the implementation raises concerns about correctness.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Hessian decomposition and missing derivation
comments.quote: Our key observation is to decompose the Hessian matrix _D_[2] _E_ ( _π_ ) into two parts ... where _H_[˜] is a _diagonal_ matrix given by _H_[˜] ( _sa_ ) _,_ ( _tb_ ) = _−τδ{_ ( _sa_ ) _,_ ( _tb_ ) _}_ ( _wππs[a]_ ) _s_
comments.explanation: The paper claims a decomposition D²E(π) = H̃ + Hr where Hr vanishes at π=π*, justifying the use of H̃ as a quasi-Newton approximation. However, no derivation is provided for this decomposition, and the form of H̃ is stated without justification. In particular, the diagonal entries depend on w_π = (I − γP_π)^{-⊤} e, which is policy-dependent, yet no explanation is given for why this captures the Hessian's diagonal. For the Shannon entropy case, the true Hessian of the regularizer includes terms like τ / π_s[a], but the given H̃ has entries −τ w_π,s π_s[a], which scales inversely. This suggests either a sign error or a confusion with the inverse Hessian. The omission is not resolved in Appendix A, which is not provided. While the connection to NPG supports the final update, the gap in derivation makes it hard to assess generalization to other entropies. This is a moderate issue because the final algorithm may still be correct, but the theoretical justification is underspecified.

comments.title: Parameterization mismatch in Algorithm 1 KL update
comments.explanation: The KL update in Algorithm 1, line 8, appears to be OCR-corrupted: '( _π_ new) _[a] s[←]_ ( _aπ_[(] _s[a][π]_[)] _s[a]_[1][)] _[−]_[1] _[η][−]_[ ex] _[η]_[exp(][p(] _[η]_[(] _[η][r]_[(] _s[a][r]_[+] _s[a]_[(][+(] _[γ][P][γP][ a][v][ a][π][v]_[)] _[π][s]_[)][)] _[s][/]_[)] _[τ][/τ]_[)]'[ suggests it should be π_new ← (1−η)π + η exp((r − (I−γP^a)v)_s / τ + c_s). But this contradicts Theorem 1, which gives θ_s[a] ← η(...) / τ + (1−η)θ_s[a] with θ_s[a] = log π_s[a], implying π_new = π^{1−η} exp(η(...)/τ). The two updates are fundamentally different—linear interpolation vs. geometric. The latter is standard for softmax parameterization. The KL divergence update should preserve positivity and normalization via exponentiated form, not linear mixing. The OCR damage makes it ambiguous, but the 'ex' followed by 'p' and 'exp(' suggests 'exp', and '−1 η−' suggests '− η' or '−1' η'. But the structure '(aπ(...)' is incomprehensible. Given that the NPG is a special case, and NPG uses exponential updates, the linear interpolation form would be incorrect. This OCR issue creates a serious ambiguity.

comments.title: Inconsistent update rule between Theorem 1 and Theorem 2
comments.explanation: The update rule in Theorem 1 includes a spurious '−τ' term: η [ r_s[a] − τ − ((I−γP^a)v_π)_s + c_s ] / τ. But τ is a scalar regularization coefficient, and subtracting it inside the reward-like term makes no sense dimensionally. The correct NPG update should involve (r − (I−γP^a)v)_s, not minus an extra τ. This term is absent in the corresponding update in Theorem 2, which correctly states θ_s[a] ← η [ r_s[a] − ((I−γP^a)v_π)_s + c_s ] / τ + (1−η)θ_s[a]. The '−τ' is likely an OCR error (perhaps '−γ' or artifact), but its presence in Theorem 1 and absence in Theorem 2 creates inconsistency. Since the Theorem 2 form is used in Algorithm 1 and numerical results, the issue may be OCR, but it remains ambiguous whether it's author error or scanning artifact. Given that the NPG special case should match known results, the '−τ' cannot be correct.

comments.title: Underspecification of bisection method in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Solve for _cs_ with the bisection method on the interval described in (16).
comments.explanation: The algorithm instructs to solve for c_s using bisection 'on the interval described in (16)', but (16) is not present in the paper—only (15) and (16) is mentioned in the context of Lemma 3, which gives an interval for the solution x. However, the specific bounds and stopping criterion for the bisection are not provided. While Lemma 3 gives a theoretical interval, a practical implementation requires details like tolerance, maximum iterations, and how the interval is initialized. More importantly, the function being solved is ∑_a (c_s + x_a)^σ = 1 with σ = 2/(α−1) < 0, which is decreasing in c_s, but numerical stability near poles (c_s ≈ −min x_a) is critical. The lack of specification makes reproduction difficult, especially since this step is essential for non-KL cases. Given the sensitivity of the update to c_s, this is a moderate concern, but other details in Lemma 3 and the structure help resolve it partially.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting quasi-Newton approach for policy gradient with entropy regularization, claiming quadratic convergence. The core idea of decomposing the Hessian into diagonal and vanishing remainder parts is elegant, and the connection to natural policy gradient for Shannon entropy is insightful. However, there are some technical issues that need clarification, particularly regarding the general entropy functions and their corresponding update schemes.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in Hessian diagonal term
comments.explanation: The notation $(w_\pi \pi_s[a])_s$ is ambiguous. If $w_\pi$ is a vector and $\pi_s[a]$ is a scalar, their product would be a vector, but the subscript $_s$ at the end is unclear. Based on the context, the authors likely mean $w_\pi[s] \cdot \pi_s[a]$, but this should be written explicitly to avoid confusion. This notation issue appears in the key Hessian decomposition that forms the foundation of the quasi-Newton method.

comments.title: Non-standard α-divergence formula
comments.explanation: The formula for α-divergence given in equation (12) doesn't match the standard definition. The standard α-divergence (for uniform prior) would have exponent $\frac{1-\alpha}{2}$ rather than $\frac{1+\alpha}{2}$ inside the parentheses. This discrepancy affects the subsequent derivations for the general entropy case, including the update scheme in equation (13). The authors should clarify whether they're using a non-standard parameterization or if this is an error.

comments.title: Unclear derivation of general entropy update
comments.explanation: The update scheme for general entropy functions in equation (13) appears without sufficient derivation. When attempting to derive this from the general framework with $\theta_s[a] = \phi'(|A|\pi_s[a])$, the resulting expression doesn't match equation (13). The derivation is crucial for understanding how the quasi-Newton method extends to non-Shannon entropies, and its absence makes it difficult to verify the correctness of the proposed algorithm for general cases.

comments.title: Strong convergence claim without clear assumptions
comments.quote: we demonstrate that the proposed quasi-Newton method typically converges in single-digit iterations, often orders of magnitude faster than other state-of-the-art algorithms
comments.explanation: The claim of single-digit convergence is very strong and appears in both the abstract and introduction. While the numerical experiments support this claim, the theoretical analysis (Theorem 5) only guarantees local quadratic convergence near the optimal policy. The paper doesn't clearly specify the basin of attraction or the conditions under which the algorithm would converge in single digits from arbitrary initialization. This could overstate the practical performance guarantees.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting quasi-Newton approach to entropy-regularized policy optimization, successfully demonstrating that a diagonal approximation of the Hessian can yield superlinear convergence in a setting where full-Hessian computation is intractable. The theoretical development and numerical experiments are coherent. However, the paper suffers from inconsistent notation in key update equations, particularly regarding the handling of constants, scalars, and subscripts, which makes it challenging to verify the transition from the general derivation to the specific algorithms presented in the pseudocode.

comments.title: Notation ambiguity in quasi-Newton update rule
comments.explanation: The formula contains an uninitialized symbol 'τ' in the denominator immediately following 'rs[a]', and then another 'τ' divisor. Specifically, the expression 'τ−[(I−γPa)]vπ[s]' appears algebraically inconsistent given the definition of the regularized objective. It is unclear if the first 'τ' is a typo for a term related to the entropy derivative or if the grouping implies a specific algebraic simplification not fully explained. While the subsequent definitions in Theorem 1 and 2 clarify the regularized reward, this specific line in Section 2 is mathematically underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Theorem 2 update expression
comments.explanation: Comparing this to (14) in Theorem 2, the expression in prose is missing a division bar or parentheses, appearing as 'rs[a]−[(I−γPa)]vπ[s]+cs/τ'. Given the context that 'θ' is related to the derivative of 'φ', it is difficult to determine if 'cs' is inside the scope of the division by 'τ'. This ambiguity is significant because the update rule is central to the claimed 'brand new algorithms' and must be precisely implemented by the reader.

comments.title: Confusing definition of alpha-divergence
comments.explanation: The formula contains a term '(1 - α^2)' followed by a summation. Standard definitions of alpha-divergence or Hellinger divergence usually involve specific power factors. The notation 'α^2' is likely a typo for 'α' or a specific parameterization, but it appears inconsistent with the later Remark 2 where setting 'α=0' leads back to the Hellinger divergence. If α=0, the formula (12) would read 'h_s = |A| * sum(1 - sqrt(|A|*pi_sa))', which differs from the standard Hellinger form by a factor. The notation is prone to misleading the reader on the exact function being optimized.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a quasi-Newton method for policy gradient with entropy regularization, extending the natural policy gradient to other divergences and demonstrating impressive empirical convergence. The theoretical claim of local quadratic convergence is supported. However, the presentation suffers from several OCR-induced notation errors and missing experimental details that may affect reproducibility. The definition of the α-divergence and the diagonal Hessian approximation are particularly unclear, and the numerical comparisons omit key hyperparameter choices for baseline methods. These issues are localized and fixable, and do not fundamentally undermine the core contribution.

comments.title: Ambiguous diagonal Hessian expression due to OCR
comments.quote: where \(\tilde{H}\) is a \(diagonal\) matrix given by \(\tilde{H}_{(sa),(tb)} = -\tau \delta_{(sa),(tb)} (w\pi\pi_s[a])_s\)
comments.explanation: The expression \((w\pi\pi_s[a])_s\) is likely an OCR artifact. From context (the quasi-Newton flow in (8) and the gradient), the intended diagonal entry should involve \((w_\pi)_s / \pi_s[a]\) so that the preconditioner yields the update in (8). Without proper typesetting, a careful reader cannot verify the decomposition. I checked the quasi-Newton flow (8) and the later update (11) which match the correct formula under this interpretation. The issue is primarily presentational but could mislead readers trying to understand the derivation.

comments.title: Unclear definition of α-divergence
comments.explanation: The given formula for the α-divergence appears dimensionally inconsistent and does not match standard definitions (e.g., for α=0 it does not reduce to the Hellinger divergence up to a constant scaling). The associated algorithm (13) may still be correct if this is a special parametrization, but the paper does not justify the constant factors or show the equivalence to the f-divergence framework mentioned earlier. This could affect the correctness of the regularization term when using the algorithm, though the numerical results suggest the updates are plausible. The authors should clarify the derivation and include a reference to the specific α-divergence form used.

comments.title: Missing hyperparameters for baseline algorithms in numerical comparison
comments.quote: In comparison, we apply the policy mirror descent (PMD) and the general policy mirror descent (GPMD) method in Zhan et al. (2021) to the same MDP with the same stopping criterion.
comments.explanation: The paper does not report the learning rates used for PMD and GPMD, only mentioning 'with manually tuned learning rate'. Without these values, the comparison is not reproducible, and the claim that the quasi-Newton method is 'orders of magnitude faster' may be sensitive to the choice of baseline hyperparameters. This is a significant omission that weakens the empirical evidence. The authors should provide the learning rates and state how they were tuned.

comments.title: Underspecified tolerance for iterative linear solver in industrial-scale experiment
comments.quote: When calculating \(v_\pi\) by \(v_\pi = (I - \gamma P_\pi)^{-1} (r_\pi - \tau h_\pi)\), we apply the iterative solver Bi-CGSTAB ...
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify the convergence tolerance or maximum iterations used for Bi-CGSTAB when solving for \(v_\pi\). The outer quasi-Newton iteration's quadratic convergence likely requires accurate evaluation of \(v_\pi\). The missing detail makes it difficult to assess the computational cost and reproducibility of the industrial-scale results. I would expect a statement like 'Bi-CGSTAB was run to a relative residual tolerance of 10^{-12}'.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in α-divergence update in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: The exponent on \(\pi_s[a]\) in the calculation of \(x_a\) is written as \((\alpha-1)/2\). In Theorem 2, equation (13) suggests the correct term is \((\pi_s[a])^{(\alpha-1)/2}\) multiplied by \((1-\eta)\). That seems consistent here. However, the presence of \(\alpha-1\) as a separate line above 'Calculate' is confusing (likely a formatting glitch). More importantly, the derivation from the general formula (13) to this step should be checked for sign consistency; the factor \(2\alpha/(1-\alpha)\) appears reasonable given the sign flip from (13). The issue is not a clear error, but the presentation is messy enough to raise doubts. Clarification would help.
